back: A directed graph G = (V, E) consists of a finite set V of vertices and a set E of ordered pairs of vertices called edges or arcs. Each edge has a specific direction from a tail to a head.
A simple digraph has no loops (edges from a vertex to itself) and no multiple edges (more than one edge from vertex u to vertex v in the same direction).
For a vertex v in a digraph, indegree (deg⁻(v)) is the number of edges entering v, and outdegree (deg⁺(v)) is the number of edges leaving v. The degree is their sum: deg(v) = deg⁻(v) + deg⁺(v).
A complete digraph on n vertices (denoted Kₙ*) is a simple digraph where for every pair of distinct vertices u and v, both directed edges (u→v) and (v→u) exist.
A tournament is a complete asymmetric digraph where for every pair of distinct vertices, exactly one directed edge exists between them. It represents the outcome of a round-robin tournament.

front: What is a Strongly Connected Digraph?
back: A digraph is strongly connected if there is a directed path from any vertex to every other vertex. Equivalently, for any two vertices u and v, there exists both a path u→v and v→u.

front: What is a Weakly Connected Digraph?
back: A digraph is weakly connected if the underlying undirected graph (ignoring edge directions) is connected. At least one path exists between any two vertices when directions are removed.

A DAG is a digraph that contains no directed cycles. This means there is no directed path that starts and ends at the same vertex. DAGs are used to represent schedules, dependencies, and topological orderings.

front: What is a Hamiltonian Path in a Digraph?
back: A Hamiltonian path visits each vertex exactly once. In a tournament, there always exists a Hamiltonian path (Redei's Theorem). A Hamiltonian cycle visits each vertex exactly once and returns to the start.
An Eulerian digraph contains a directed Eulerian circuit (closed trail using all edges exactly once) if and only if it is strongly connected and every vertex has equal indegree and outdegree.

front: What is the Adjacency Matrix of a Digraph?
back: An n×n matrix A where A[i][j] = 1 if there is a directed edge from vertex i to vertex j, and 0 otherwise. For weighted digraphs, the entry contains the weight.

The transitive closure of a digraph G is a digraph G* that contains an edge from u to v if and only if there exists a directed path from u to v in G. It can be computed using Floyd-Warshall algorithm.
A regular digraph is one where all vertices have the same outdegree (and consequently the same indegree if the digraph is regular). A k-regular digraph has outdegree k for every vertex.
Two digraphs G₁ and G₂ are isomorphic if there exists a bijection f: V(G₁) → V(G₂) such that (u,v) is an edge in G₁ if and only if (f(u), f(v)) is an edge in G₂.

front: What is a Complete Bipartite Digraph?
back: A complete bipartite digraph K_{m,n}* has vertex partition (X,Y) where |X|=m, |Y|=n, and every vertex in X has arcs to every vertex in Y, and every vertex in Y has arcs to every vertex in X.
